unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8! This unit is all about homes. You will learn the names of different kinds of homes for people and for animals — a house, an apartment, a hut, a tree house, a nest, a hive, and a hole. You will name the rooms inside a home and the things we keep in them. You will learn how homes look different around the world, and how to say where things are using words like in, on, and under. You will also talk about the jobs you do to help at home. By the end, you will be able to give a little tour of your own home, just like Selma does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher audiovisual lecture and learn a few vocabulary words each day.
Complete all six grammar practices, moving from guided examples to independent use.
Listen to the Unit readings, including Helping Hands at Home, and answer the comprehension questions.
Complete all six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question quiz and student self-assessment.

visual.alt: A warm family home showing rooms, furniture and household jobs

readings.title: A Poem: “Homes”
readings.passageScript:
Read this little poem out loud:
A nest is a home for a bird.
A hive is a home for a bee.
A hole is a home for a rabbit.
And a house is a home for me.

readings.title: Different Kinds of Homes
readings.passageScript:
Read this text:
People live in many different kinds of homes around the world. In cities, many people live in apartments. An apartment is a set of rooms that is part of a big building. Some people live in houses with a garden. In some hot, dry places, people build houses from mud and dry grass, called adobe houses. The thick mud walls keep them cool. In some hot, wet places, people build houses high on stilts so the air can blow underneath. Every home is special because it keeps people safe, warm and dry.

readings.title: Nadia's Tree House
readings.passageScript:
Listen to Nadia. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“My name is Nadia. My father built a tree house in our garden. It is like a small house in a tree! It has walls, a roof and a small window. To get up, you climb a ladder. The tree house has two floors. I love playing there with my friends.”

readings.title: Helping Hands at Home
readings.theme: Rooms in a home, things at home, and helping your family.
readings.setting: A family home in a Somali town, on the day before a visit from Grandma.
readings.passageScript:
Selma lived in a small house near the market with her mother, her father, and her little brother Ishmael. The house had two bedrooms, a kitchen, a bathroom, and a cosy living room with a bright red rug on the floor.
One sunny morning, Mother had good news. “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said. “She will stay for one whole week!”
“Ayeeyo is coming!” shouted Ishmael, jumping up and down.
Selma smiled, but then she looked around. There were toys under the sofa. There were dishes in the sink. There was dust on the little table by the window. The house was not ready for a special guest.
“Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother. “But I cannot do it all by myself. Will you help me?”
“I will help!” said Selma.
“Me too!” said Ishmael.
Mother clapped her hands happily. “Then we are a team,” she said. “Let us start.”
First, they went to the bedroom. “I will make my bed,” said Selma. She pulled the blanket flat and put her pillow at the top. Then she put her clean clothes away in the closet. Ishmael picked up his toys from under the bed and put them in the toy box.
“Look, Selma!” he said. “Now the floor is clear!”
Next, they went to the living room. Selma swept the floor with the big broom. She shook the red rug outside so it was clean again. Ishmael wiped the little table until the dust was gone.
“Where should I put this book?” asked Ishmael.
“Put it on the shelf, next to the other books,” said Selma. Ishmael placed it neatly on the shelf.
In the kitchen, Mother was cooking sweet rice and sambuus for tomorrow. The kitchen smelled wonderful. Selma washed the dishes in the sink, and Ishmael dried them with a cloth. Then Selma set the table with clean plates and cups.
“You are working so hard,” said Father, coming in from the garden. “I have news too. Look outside the window!”
The children ran to the window. In the garden, under the big tree, Father had built something new. It had walls, a small roof, and a tiny window.
“A tree house!” cried Selma. “For us?”
“Yes,” laughed Father. “When your work is done, you may climb up and play. But first, we finish helping your mother.”
So Selma and Ishmael worked until every room was tidy. The bedroom was neat. The living room was clean. There were fresh flowers on the table, and the whole house felt warm and welcoming.
The next day, Ayeeyo arrived. She looked around the little house and smiled a big smile.
“What a beautiful home,” she said. “It is clean, it is warm, and it is full of love. Who helped to make it so lovely?”
“We did!” said Selma and Ishmael together.
Ayeeyo hugged them both. “A home is not just walls and a roof,” she said softly. “A home is a place where a family helps each other. Thank you, my dear helping hands.”
That evening, Selma and Ishmael climbed the ladder to their new tree house. They looked down at their little house, warm and bright below them.
“Home, sweet home,” said Selma happily. And she felt very glad that she had helped.

comprehension.question: What is a home for a bird? For a bee? For a rabbit?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: A hive is a home for a bee. A hole is a home for a rabbit.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: A hive is a home for a bee. A hole is a home for a rabbit.

comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: A hive is a home for a bee.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: A hive is a home for a bee.

comprehension.question: Can you think of another animal and its home to add to the poem?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: A hive is a home for a bee.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: A hive is a home for a bee.

comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: In cities, many people live in apartments. Some people live in houses with a garden.

comprehension.question: What are adobe houses made of? Why do the thick walls help?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: In some hot, dry places, people build houses from mud and dry grass, called adobe houses. The thick mud walls keep them cool.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: In some hot, dry places, people build houses from mud and dry grass, called adobe houses. The thick mud walls keep them cool.

comprehension.question: What kind of home do you live in?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: In cities, many people live in apartments.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: In cities, many people live in apartments.

comprehension.question: Who built the tree house? Where is it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Nadia's father built the tree house, and it is in their garden.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

comprehension.question: How do you get up to the tree house?
comprehension.correctAnswer: You climb a ladder to get up to the tree house.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

comprehension.question: How many floors does it have?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: The tree house has two floors.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: The tree house has two floors.

comprehension.question: Would you like a tree house? Why or why not?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.

comprehension.question: Who is coming to visit, and for how long?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said.

comprehension.question: Name three rooms in Selma's house.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Selma lived in a small house near the market with her mother, her father, and her little brother Ishmael.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Selma lived in a small house near the market with her mother, her father, and her little brother Ishmael.

comprehension.question: What job did Selma do in the bedroom? What did Ishmael do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Selma lived in a small house near the market with her mother, her father, and her little brother Ishmael. Selma washed the dishes in the sink, and Ishmael dried them with a cloth.

comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “You are working so hard,” said Father, coming in from the garden. In the garden, under the big tree, Father had built something new.

comprehension.question: What did Ayeeyo say a home really is?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother.

comprehension.question: Why did the house need to be tidied before Ayeeyo came? How does a clean, welcoming home make a guest feel?

comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “A home is a place where a family helps each other. “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother. “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said. Will you help me?” “I will help!” said Selma.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “A home is a place where a family helps each other. “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother. “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said. Will you help me?” “I will help!” said Selma.

comprehension.question: How did Selma feel at the end of the story? Why do you think helping made her feel glad?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.

grammar.title: Prepositions of Place
grammar.explanation: Prepositions of place are small words that tell us where something is. They help us describe our home so another person can picture it — where the bed is, where your shoes are, where the picture hangs.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
in — inside something:  “The bed is in the bedroom.”
on — touching the top of something:  “The book is on the table.”
under — below something:  “The shoes are under the bed.”
next to — beside something:  “The chair is next to the window.”
between — in the middle of two things:  “The lamp is between the two books.”
above — higher than something:  “The picture is above the sofa.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The book is in the table.”  ✗
Fix: The book is touching the top of the table, so we say on: “The book is on the table.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Point as you say it! Put your hand IN a box, ON a box, and UNDER a box. Your hand helps your brain remember the word.
grammar.practice:
Fill in the blank with: in, on, under, next to, between, or above.
The rug is ______ the floor.
The clothes are ______ the closet.
The cat is ______ the chair (hiding below it).
The window is ______ the bed (higher up on the wall).
The little table is ______ the two beds.

grammar.title: Prepositions of Place: Use It
grammar.explanation: Prepositions of place are small words that tell us where something is. They help us describe our home so another person can picture it — where the bed is, where your shoes are, where the picture hangs. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 8 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
in — inside something:  “The bed is in the bedroom.”
on — touching the top of something:  “The book is on the table.”
under — below something:  “The shoes are under the bed.”
next to — beside something:  “The chair is next to the window.”
between — in the middle of two things:  “The lamp is between the two books.”
above — higher than something:  “The picture is above the sofa.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The book is in the table.”  ✗
Fix: The book is touching the top of the table, so we say on: “The book is on the table.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Point as you say it! Put your hand IN a box, ON a box, and UNDER a box. Your hand helps your brain remember the word.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: There is / There are
grammar.explanation: We use “there is” and “there are” to say that something can be found in a place. This is perfect for describing a home: what is in each room.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use there is with ONE thing (a singular noun).
There is a bed in the bedroom.
There is a kitchen in my house.
There is a bird in the nest.
Use there are with MORE THAN ONE thing (a plural noun, usually ending in “s”).
There are two windows in my room.
There are three bedrooms in the apartment.
There are books on the shelf.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “There is two chairs in the kitchen.”  ✗
Fix: “Two chairs” is more than one, so we say there are: “There are two chairs in the kitchen.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: One thing → “there is”. Many things → “there are”. If you can hear an “s” on the thing (chairs, beds, windows), use “are”.
grammar.practice:
Write is or are.
There ______ a sofa in the living room.
There ______ four chairs at the table.
There ______ a rug on the floor.
There ______ two windows in the kitchen.
There ______ a toilet in the bathroom.

grammar.title: There is / There are: Use It
grammar.explanation: We use “there is” and “there are” to say that something can be found in a place. This is perfect for describing a home: what is in each room. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 8 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use there is with ONE thing (a singular noun).
There is a bed in the bedroom.
There is a kitchen in my house.
There is a bird in the nest.
Use there are with MORE THAN ONE thing (a plural noun, usually ending in “s”).
There are two windows in my room.
There are three bedrooms in the apartment.
There are books on the shelf.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “There is two chairs in the kitchen.”  ✗
Fix: “Two chairs” is more than one, so we say there are: “There are two chairs in the kitchen.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: One thing → “there is”. Many things → “there are”. If you can hear an “s” on the thing (chairs, beds, windows), use “are”.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Talking About the Future with “will”
grammar.explanation: We use will to talk about something that has not happened yet — a plan or an idea for later. In this unit you use it to plan things, like a playground or a job you will do at home.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use will + a verb (the same for I, you, he, she, we, they).
I will tidy my room after lunch.
We will have a slide in our playground.
She will help her mother cook.
To say NO, use will not — its short form is won't.
I will not forget to make my bed.
It won't rain today.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I will to help my father.”  ✗
Fix: Do not put “to” after will. Say: “I will help my father.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: will = a promise about later. Think: “I will, I will, tomorrow I will!” Just add the plain verb, with no “to”.
grammar.practice:
Make each sentence about the future. Use “will” + the verb in brackets.
Tomorrow I ______ (sweep) the floor.
After dinner we ______ (wash) the dishes.
She ______ (set) the table tonight.
They ______ (build) a tree house in the garden.

grammar.title: Talking About the Future with “will”: Use It
grammar.explanation: We use will to talk about something that has not happened yet — a plan or an idea for later. In this unit you use it to plan things, like a playground or a job you will do at home. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 8 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use will + a verb (the same for I, you, he, she, we, they).
I will tidy my room after lunch.
We will have a slide in our playground.
She will help her mother cook.
To say NO, use will not — its short form is won't.
I will not forget to make my bed.
It won't rain today.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I will to help my father.”  ✗
Fix: Do not put “to” after will. Say: “I will help my father.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: will = a promise about later. Think: “I will, I will, tomorrow I will!” Just add the plain verb, with no “to”.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe a Kind of Home
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose a home: a house, an apartment, a hut, a tree house, a nest, a hive or a hole. Then say:
“This is a ______.”
“It is made of ______.”
“______ live in this home.”
“It has ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the home? Did you say what it is made of and who lives there?

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Describe a Room
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Think about the rooms in a home: bedroom, kitchen, bathroom, living room, dining room. For each room, say:
“This is the ______.”
“People ______ in this room.”
“In this room there is / there are ______.”

speaking.title: Speaking Game — My Home Tour
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Give a tour of your home to your AI tutor or your family:
Walk from room to room and say: “This is my ______.”
Say what you do there: “In this room, we ______.”
Say what is in it: “There is / There are ______.”
End with your favourite: “I like this room because ______.”

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Home Tour
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Guide a listener through six parts of a home and explain what people do in each room.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use there is, there are and clear place words.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Where Does It Belong?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Describe the position of six household objects for a partner to identify.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use in, on, under, next to, between and above.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - Helping at Home Plan
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain three jobs you do now and three jobs you will do later.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use will for future actions and speak politely.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 1 — A Paragraph About Your Home
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short paragraph about your home. Try to answer these questions:
What kind of home do you live in?
How many rooms does it have?
Who do you live with?
What is your favourite room, and why?
Example: “I live in a small house with my family. Our house has three bedrooms, a kitchen, a living room and a bathroom. My favourite room is the living room because we all sit together there in the evening.”
writing.modelText: I live in a warm home with my family. There is a table in the kitchen. I help by sweeping and putting things away.
writing.sentenceStarter: I live in a warm home with my family.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Your Own Animal Homes Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four lines like the “Homes” poem. Start each line with the home, and end with the animal:
A ______ is a home for a ______. (for example: A den is a home for a fox.)
Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
writing.modelText:
A nest is a home for a bird.
A hive is a home for a bee.
A den is a home for a fox.
And my house is a home for me.
writing.sentenceStarter: A nest is a home for a bird.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Story response: Helping Hands at Home
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about jobs you do to help at home. Start with: “At home, I ______.”
Draw your favourite room in your home. Write one sentence using “There is” and one using “There are”.
writing.modelText: Selma and Ishmael prepared the home for Ayeeyo. They swept, tidied and worked as a team. Helping made their home feel warm and welcoming.
writing.sentenceStarter: Selma and Ishmael prepared the home for Ayeeyo.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 4 - My Home Description
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six sentences describing the kind of home, rooms and important objects in it.
writing.modelText: I live in an apartment. There are three rooms. Our table is in the living room next to the window.
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 5 - Homes Around the World
writing.promptAndInstructions: Compare two kinds of homes in five sentences, including one similarity and two differences.
writing.modelText: A hut and an apartment are both homes. A hut may have one level, while an apartment can be high above the ground.
writing.sentenceStarter: A hut and an apartment are both homes
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.

writing.title: Writing 6 - My Helping-at-Home Promise
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six sentences about jobs you will do to help at home this week.
writing.modelText: I will make my bed. I will put my books away and help clean the table after dinner.

activities.title: Activity 1 — Match Homes and Who Lives There
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Draw a line from each home to who or what lives in it.
house   →   ______
apartment   →   ______
tree house   →   ______
nest   →   ______
hive   →   ______
hole   →   ______
activities.answerSummary: house → a family;  apartment → city people;  tree house → children (for play);  nest → birds;  hive → bees;  hole → rabbits.

activities.title: Activity 2 — Match Rooms and Activities
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each room with what people do there.
bedroom   →   ______
kitchen   →   ______
bathroom   →   ______
living room   →   ______
dining room   →   ______
activities.answerSummary: bedroom → sleeping;  kitchen → cooking;  bathroom → washing;  living room → relaxing;  dining room → eating.

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Preposition
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill in each blank with: in, on, under, next to, between, or above.
The bed is ______ the bedroom.
The book is ______ the table.
The shoes are ______ the bed.
The chair is ______ the window.
The picture is ______ the sofa.
activities.answerSummary: 1. in   2. on   3. under   4. next to   5. above

activities.title: Activity 4 — True or False (Home Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
Birds build nests to lay eggs.
Bees live in holes in the ground.
A tree house is built in the branches of a tree.
A hut is usually a big building with many rooms.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True  2. False (bees live in hives)  3. True  4. False (a hut is small, with one or two rooms)

activities.title: Activity 5 - Room Sort
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve household objects into the room where each is usually used.
activities.answerSummary: Answers vary. Require complete, relevant work that follows every instruction and uses the target Unit language accurately.
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

activities.title: Activity 6 - Build a Home Map
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw a simple home plan and write six position sentences about rooms or objects.
activities.answerSummary: Answers vary. Require complete, relevant work that follows every instruction and uses the target Unit language accurately.

assignments.title: Home, Sweet Home learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Complete 'Writing 1 — A Paragraph About Your Home' and record 'Speaking 1 — Describe a Kind of Home'. Submit both pieces after checking the writing and speaking rubrics.

quizzes.question: What does 'house' mean?
quizzes.options: A building where people live, with walls and a roof. | A set of rooms to live in inside a bigger building. | A small, simple house with only one or two rooms. | A small house built up in the branches of a tree.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A building where people live, with walls and a roof.
quizzes.explanation: My family lives in a house with a garden.

quizzes.question: Which word means: A set of rooms to live in inside a bigger building.
quizzes.explanation: In the city, many people live in an apartment.

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'hut'?
quizzes.explanation: In the village, some people live in a hut made of mud and straw.

quizzes.question: The children climb a ladder to their ____.
quizzes.explanation: The children climb a ladder to their tree house.

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: The bird made a nest in the tall tree.

quizzes.question: Which word means: A home for bees.
quizzes.explanation: hive: A home for bees. The bees make honey in their hive.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which word means: An opening in the ground where some animals live.
quizzes.explanation: hole: An opening in the ground where some animals live. The rabbit ran into its hole.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'bedroom'?
quizzes.explanation: bedroom: A room where people sleep. I keep my bed and toys in my bedroom.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: My mother is cooking rice in the ____.
quizzes.explanation: kitchen: A room where food is cooked. My mother is cooking rice in the kitchen.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: bathroom: A room with a sink and a toilet for washing. I wash my hands in the bathroom.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

teacherNotes.note:
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the ‘Homes’ poem with me and help me write a new line?” “What kinds of homes do people live in around the world?” “Can you play a listening game about rooms in a home?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me practise describing the rooms in my home?” “What details should I add to make my home tour interesting?” “Can you check if my descriptions are correct?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check my paragraph about my home?” “Are the animal homes in my poem correct?”
Story: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?” “Can you check my sentences about helping at home?” “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?”
Grammar: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check my grammar answers?” “When do I use ‘there is’ and when do I use ‘there are’?” “Can you give me five more sentences with prepositions of place?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “What kinds of homes are common in East Africa?” “What other animals build nests?” “Can you quiz me on the types of homes?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “What other rooms might a home have?” “What is the difference between a living room and a dining room?” “Can you quiz me on the rooms in a home?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “In which room would I find each of these things?” “Can you help me learn more words for things at home?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you ask me which jobs I do at home?” “How can I be more helpful at home?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Why do adobe houses have thick walls?” “Can you show me traditional homes from East Africa?”

Term placement approved. Outcome mappings, comprehension guidance, vocabulary enrichment, assessments, live sessions, rubrics, writing specifications and answer-key coverage completed in the approved reference model.

bedroom → sleeping;  kitchen → cooking;  bathroom → washing;  living room → relaxing;  dining room → eating.
1. in   2. on   3. under   4. next to   5. above

1. on   2. in   3. under   4. above   5. between
1. is   2. are   3. is   4. are   5. is

Tomorrow I will sweep the floor.
After dinner we will wash the dishes.
She will set the table tonight.
They will build a tree house in the garden.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: A hive is a home for a bee. A hole is a home for a rabbit.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: A hive is a home for a bee. A hole is a home for a rabbit.

Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: In cities, many people live in apartments. Some people live in houses with a garden.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: In some hot, dry places, people build houses from mud and dry grass, called adobe houses. The thick mud walls keep them cool.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: In some hot, dry places, people build houses from mud and dry grass, called adobe houses. The thick mud walls keep them cool.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: In cities, many people live in apartments.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: In cities, many people live in apartments.

Nadia's father built the tree house, and it is in their garden.
Marking guidance: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

You climb a ladder to get up to the tree house.
Marking guidance: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: The tree house has two floors.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: The tree house has two floors.

Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
Marking guidance: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Selma lived in a small house near the market with her mother, her father, and her little brother Ishmael.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Selma lived in a small house near the market with her mother, her father, and her little brother Ishmael.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Selma lived in a small house near the market with her mother, her father, and her little brother Ishmael. Selma washed the dishes in the sink, and Ishmael dried them with a cloth.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “You are working so hard,” said Father, coming in from the garden. In the garden, under the big tree, Father had built something new.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “You are working so hard,” said Father, coming in from the garden. In the garden, under the big tree, Father had built something new.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: The house was not ready for a special guest. “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: The house was not ready for a special guest. “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “A home is a place where a family helps each other. “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother. “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said. Will you help me?” “I will help!” said Selma.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “A home is a place where a family helps each other. “Our home must look nice for Ayeeyo,” said Mother. “Ayeeyo — your grandma — will visit us tomorrow,” she said. Will you help me?” “I will help!” said Selma.

Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
Marking guidance: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.
Correct answer: A building where people live, with walls and a roof.. My family lives in a house with a garden.
Correct answer: apartment. In the city, many people live in an apartment.
Correct answer: Noun. In the village, some people live in a hut made of mud and straw.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: hive. hive: A home for bees. The bees make honey in their hive.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: hole. hole: An opening in the ground where some animals live. The rabbit ran into its hole.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Noun. bedroom: A room where people sleep. I keep my bed and toys in my bedroom.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: kitchen. kitchen: A room where food is cooked. My mother is cooking rice in the kitchen.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: bathroom. bathroom: A room with a sink and a toilet for washing. I wash my hands in the bathroom.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

selfAssessment.statement: I can name different kinds of homes for people and animals.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the rooms in a home and say what people do there.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name things at home and say which room they belong in.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use prepositions of place (in, on, under…).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use “there is” and “there are”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about the future with “will”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can describe my own home.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support
